title: Characterization of electrolytically prepared brushite and hydroxyapatite coatings on orthopedic alloys
abstract:
Characterization of electrolytically prepared brushite and 
hydroxyapatite coatings on orthopedic alloys 
Jody Redepenning,l.* Tom Schlessinger,' Sandra B~rnham,~ 
Louis lip pi ell^,^ and John Miyano5 
'Department of Chemistry, University of Nebraska, Lincoln, Nebraska 68588-0304; 'University of Colorado Medical 
School, Denver, Colorado 80220; 3Department of Chemistry, Stanford University, Stanford, California 94305; 
'Harrington Arthritis Research Center, Phoenix, Arizona 85006; 5University of Nebraska Medical Center, 
Omaha, Nebraska 681 05 
Details of a procedure for electrolytically preparing highly 
pure brushite (CaHPO, . 2H,O) coatings on high surface 
area metal substrates are provided. The influence of current 
density and deposition time on coating morphology is de- 
scribed. After a discussion of procedures used to convert 
the brushite coatings to hydroxyapatite, results from a pre- 
liminary animal study that demonstrate the propensity of 
these coatings to stimulate bony ingrowth into porous 
plugs are presented. 0 1996 John Wiley & Sons, Inc. 
INTRODUCTION 
It is well documented that calcium phosphate coat- 
ings can augment the rate of bone growth to metallic 
implants, such as joint 
Long-term 
studies indicate that there is a statistically significant 
difference between both the amount of bone growth 
and the strength with which bone adheres to coated 
implants versus uncoated implants." Stimulation of 
bone growth has been shown to be particularly high 
for hydroxyapatite coatings. Although coatings pro- 
duced by plasma spraying can be unsatisfactory in 
several respects, it is the most common method used 
to deposit hydroxyapatite coatings on metallic im- 
plants. Thermal degradation of the metallic surface to 
be coated and thermal degradation of the hydroxy- 
apatite itself is frequently observed. The composition 
and crystallinity of the coatings are frequently not 
reproducible." Additionally, the "hydroxyapatite" 
materials from which the coatings are prepared vary 
from manufacturer to manufacturer, and the compo- 
sition sometimes varies from one lot number to an- 
other for a given manufacturer." Consequently, coat- 
ings with a distribution of chemical and physical 
properties may result. 
We sought to devise a relatively simple, low tem- 
perature technique for reproducibly depositing cal- 
cium phosphates on metal surfaces. We desired a 
*To whom correspondence should be addressed. 
non-line-of-sight procedure that could be used to uni- 
formly coat irregularly shaped objects. It also was 
hoped that the procedure could be used to maintain a 
high degree of control over deposit crystallinity. The 
general procedure devised to meet these goals was 
first described in a communication to Chemistry of 
Materials during 1990.12 Subsequent to our work, 
Shirkhanzadeh et al. published several short papers 
demonstrating that similar results could be achieved 
using the same 
Detailed below are our 
recent efforts to optimize the coating conditions nec- 
essary to electrolytically produce highly pure brushite 
(CaHPO, * 2H20) coatings on metallic substrates. 
Qualitative observations are provided in addition to 
quantitative measurements. Conditions necessary to 
convert the brushite coatings to hydroxyapatite are 
also described. Finally, preliminary animal studies 
that demonstrate the propensity of these coatings to 
promote bony ingrowth into porous metallic plugs 
are reported. 
MATERIALS AND METHODS 
Optimization and characterization of coatings 
Materials and equipment 
Journal of Biomedical Materials Research, Vol. 30, 287-294 (1996) 
0 1996 John Wiley & Sons, Inc. 
All brushite coatings were prepared as described 
previously.12 Saturated solutions of Ca(H,P04), were 
CCC 0021-9304/96/030287-08 
 288 
REDEPENNING ET AL. 
typically prepared by adding 15 g of Ca(H,PO,), 
(Sigma) to 500 mL of distilled/deionized water. The 
solutions were then stirred vigorously for approxi- 
mately 30 min to assure saturation and then filtered 
through a medium-porosity glass frit to remove sus- 
pended monobasic calcium phosphate crystals. The 
resulting filtrate was used as the electrolyte solution 
for subsequent electrochemical depositions. Sodium 
hydroxide solutions were prepared by dissolving 
NaOH pellets (EM Science) in distilled/deionized wa- 
ter. A PAR Model 273 potentiostatigalvanostat was 
used to control the current during the depositions. 
The device to be coated was used as the cathode, and 
a platinum basket was used as the anode. Because the 
potential drop near the coated device is dominated by 
resistive effects, the potential was not typically mon- 
itored during the depositions. X-ray powder diffrac- 
tion patterns were obtained using a Rigaku D/Max I1 
B automated X-ray diffractometer. 
Results and discussion 
The synthetic sequence used to produce calcium 
phosphate coatings on metallic surfaces is a combi- 
nation of electrochemical half reactions, acid-base re- 
actions, and precipitation reactions. First, water is re- 
duced at the cathode surface (i.e., at the surface of the 
prosthesis) to produce hydrogen gas and hydroxide 
ions, as shown in the following half reaction: 
2 H,O + 2e- = H, + 2 OH- 
(1) 
Thus, by controlling the current that passes, the pH is 
controlled at the surface of the device. The hydroxide 
ions generated at the surface then may react with 
dihydrogen phosphate according to the equilibrium 
shown below: 
OH- + H2P04- 
H2O + HP0:- 
(2) 
The following equilibrium for precipitation of 
(CaHPO, . 2H,O), brushite, is relevant in the pres- 
ence of calcium ions: 
Ca2+ + HP0;- 
+ 2H20 e 
CaHPO, . 2H,O (3) 
Because the current density controls the pH at the 
surface, because the pH determines the position of 
the equilibrium in Equation (2), and because the con- 
centration of HP0,z 
influences the position of the 
equilibrium in Equation (3) and the rate at which 
CaHPO, . 2H,O is deposited, the deposition rate and 
the deposit morphology can be controlled by control- 
ling the current density. 
Although brushite coatings may be useful them- 
selves, the biomaterials community has understand- 
ably focused on hydroxyapatite coatings. It is fortu- 
nate that brushite coatings can be produced electro- 
lytically because brushite is an important synthetic 
intermediate to preparing highly pure hydroxyapa- 
tite. Chromatographers have known for many years 
that it is difficult to prepare hydroxyapatite that is 
pure and has the proper morphology to provide de- 
sired flow rates. Direct precipitation of hydroxyapa- 
tite leads to small crystals that pack so tightly in chro- 
matographic columns that solvent flow becomes pro- 
hibitively slow; however, the crystal size of brushite 
can be controlled by homogeneous precipitation, and 
once brushite with the proper morphology has been 
obtained, a solid to solid transformation of brushite to 
hydroxyapatite can be made. This transformation oc- 
curs according to the following equilibrium: 
5 CaHPO, . 2H20 + 6 OH- e 
Ca,(PO,),OH 
+ 2PO4,- + 15 H,O 
(4) 
M.W. Neuman has demonstrated that brushite is not 
stable above pH 6.9 and that above this pH it is con- 
verted to hydr~xyapatite.l~-’~ 
At neutral pH this re- 
action occurs relatively slowly, however. Conse- 
quently, we have opted to modify the procedure of 
Tiselius, Hjerten, and Levine,” which describes con- 
version of brushite to chromatographically useful hy- 
droxyapatite, to meet our needs. 
Because the propensity of the calcium phosphate 
coatings to promote bone growth to prosthetic de- 
vices ultimately will be influenced by the coating 
thickness and morphology, we performed a series of 
experiments to ascertain how coating thickness and 
morphology can be controlled. Previous experience 
had suggested that it was possible to control the mor- 
phology by controlling the current density and that 
coating thickness could be controlled by controlling 
the deposition time. We will focus first on the rela- 
tionships between the current density, the total 
amount of charge passed, and the current efficiency. 
Figure 1 shows plots of current efficiency versus 
current density for total charges ranging from 1 to 20 
C/cm2. At very low current densities the current effi- 
ciency drops off precipitously. One might speculate 
that this is due to the fact that at low current densities 
the amount of hydroxide produced at the interface is 
small. Because of the large buffering capacity pro- 
vided by H2P04-, little pH change should occur at 
the interface, and one might be led to believe that 
little CaHPO, 
2H20 should be deposited. This is 
probably not a valid explanation, however. The cur- 
rent efficiency is calculated assuming that a current 
efficiency of 100% corresponds to the case when one 
mole of CaHPO, . 2H20 is deposited for each mole of 
electrons (mole of hydroxide) introduced. When a 
saturated solution of Ca(H,PO,), is prepared, the re- 
sulting solution is acidic (- pH 3.5). At this pH the 
reaction shown in Equation (2) should proceed nearly

All brushite coatings were prepared as described
previously.12 Saturated solutions of Ca(H,P04), were
typically prepared by adding 15 g of Ca(H,PO,),
(Sigma) to 500 mL of distilled/deionized water. The
solutions were then stirred vigorously for approxi-
mately 30 min to assure saturation and then filtered
through a medium-porosity glass frit to remove sus-
pended monobasic calcium phosphate crystals. The
resulting filtrate was used as the electrolyte solution
for subsequent electrochemical depositions. Sodium
hydroxide solutions were prepared by dissolving
NaOH pellets (EM Science) in distilled/deionized wa-
ter. A PAR Model 273 potentiostatigalvanostat was
used to control the current during the depositions.
The device to be coated was used as the cathode, and
a platinum basket was used as the anode. Because the
potential drop near the coated device is dominated by
resistive effects, the potential was not typically mon-
itored during the depositions. X-ray powder diffrac-
tion patterns were obtained using a Rigaku D/Max I1
B automated X-ray diffractometer.

phosphate coatings on metallic surfaces is a combi-
nation of electrochemical half reactions, acid-base re-
actions, and precipitation reactions. First, water is re-

prosthesis) to produce hydrogen gas and hydroxide

Although brushite coatings may be useful them-
selves, the biomaterials community has understand-
ably focused on hydroxyapatite coatings. It is fortu-
nate that brushite coatings can be produced electro-
lytically because brushite is an important synthetic
intermediate to preparing highly pure hydroxyapa-
tite. Chromatographers have known for many years
that it is difficult to prepare hydroxyapatite that is
pure and has the proper morphology to provide de-
sired flow rates. Direct precipitation of hydroxyapa-
tite leads to small crystals that pack so tightly in chro-
matographic columns that solvent flow becomes pro-
hibitively slow; however, the crystal size of brushite
can be controlled by homogeneous precipitation, and
once brushite with the proper morphology has been
obtained, a solid to solid transformation of brushite to
hydroxyapatite can be made. This transformation oc-
curs according to the following equilibrium:
M.W. Neuman has demonstrated that brushite is not
stable above pH 6.9 and that above this pH it is con-
verted to hydr~xyapatite.l~-’~
action occurs relatively slowly, however. Conse-
quently, we have opted to modify the procedure of
Tiselius, Hjerten, and Levine,” which describes con-
version of brushite to chromatographically useful hy-
droxyapatite, to meet our needs.
Because the propensity of the calcium phosphate
coatings to promote bone growth to prosthetic de-
vices ultimately will be influenced by the coating
thickness and morphology, we performed a series of
experiments to ascertain how coating thickness and
morphology can be controlled. Previous experience
had suggested that it was possible to control the mor-
phology by controlling the current density and that
coating thickness could be controlled by controlling
the deposition time. We will focus first on the rela-
tionships between the current density, the total
amount of charge passed, and the current efficiency.
Figure 1 shows plots of current efficiency versus
current density for total charges ranging from 1 to 20
C/cm2. At very low current densities the current effi-
ciency drops off precipitously. One might speculate
that this is due to the fact that at low current densities
the amount of hydroxide produced at the interface is
small. Because of the large buffering capacity pro-
vided by H2P04-, little pH change should occur at
the interface, and one might be led to believe that
2H20 should be deposited. This is
probably not a valid explanation, however. The cur-
rent efficiency is calculated assuming that a current
efficiency of 100% corresponds to the case when one
mole of CaHPO, . 2H20 is deposited for each mole of
electrons (mole of hydroxide) introduced. When a
saturated solution of Ca(H,PO,), is prepared, the re-
sulting solution is acidic (- pH 3.5). At this pH the
reaction shown in Equation (2) should proceed nearly
BRUSHITE AND HYDROXYAPATITE COATINGS
sity for different total amounts of charge passed.
Current efficiency as a function of current den-
stoichiometrically from the left to right. Nearly every
OH- produced should react with a H,PO,-
duce a HP0,2-. Thus, when the current density is
low, the HP0Z-/H2PO4- ratio will be small, and lit-
tle pH change will be observed, even though the ef-
ficiency of HP0,2- production is high. Conse-
quently, it is not necessary for there to be a large pH
change in order to get a high current efficiency for
production. If the efficiency of HPOZ- pro-
duction is high at low current densities, why is the
efficiency of CaHPO, . 2H,O deposition low under
these conditions? One plausible explanation is that
the product of the concentrations of HP0;-
Ca2+ never exceeds the K,, for CaHPO, * 2H20, and,
hence, that precipitation of CaHPO, . 2H20 is ther-
modynamically unfavorable. Alternatively, slow ki-
netics for the precipitation reaction would permit the
products from the electrolysis, i.e., the reactants for
the precipitation, to diffuse away from the surface
before precipitation could occur. Unfortunately, our
ability to visually monitor the reaction is limited at
low current densities due to the slow rate at which
relatively small amounts of material are produced.
Whatever the explanation for the low current efficien-
cies at low current densities actually is, it is important
to note that coatings formed at low current densities
are probably not of much practical utility. As was
shown in earlier work," CaHPO, . 2H20 coatings
produced at low current densities exhibit undesirable
morphologies. The coatings contain large fragile crys-
tals of CaHPO, . 2H,O. Additionally, because of the
low current density and low current efficiency, very
long deposition times are required to build up coat-
ings of sufficient thicknesses.
The fall off in current efficiency at high current den-
sities is easier to interpret. At high current density
vigorous hydrogen evolution is observed. Conse-
quently, convective mass transport of reactants and
product becomes dominant, and significant amounts
of CaHPO, * 2H20 are lost to solution. The appear-
ance of CaHPO, . 2H20 in the bulk solution can be
attributed to two mechanisms. One possibility is that
convection causes nucleation and crystal growth to be
initiated in solution rather than directly on the sur-
face of the device. This does not appear to be the
dominant mechanism, however. At high current den-
sities it is visually apparent that much of the
CaHPO, * 2H20 that reaches solution is physically
disrupted from the surface by hydrogen evolution.
Although the disruption lowers the overall efficiency,
it is advantageous because the physical integrity of
the coating is improved. A type of Darwinian natural
selection similar to that observed by Mareese in ul-
trasonically agitated media occurs.z1 Weakly attached
crystals cannot survive the turbulent environment
near the surface; thus, growth occurs preferentially at
those nucleation sites that are strongly attached to the
surface of the device being coated.
Another feature worth noting in Figure 1 is that the
current efficiency generally decreases as the total
amount of charge passed increases. At any chosen
current density, the current efficiency is higher for
thin films than for thick films. This effect is readily
apparent if the data in Figure 1 are plotted as is
shown in Figures 2a and 2b. The effect occurs because
the deposits are prepared at constant current. As the
coatings begin to form, portions of the electrode sur-
face become passivated, and more current is forced
through increasingly smaller regions that have not
yet become passivated. When depositions are per-
formed at high current densities for very long periods
of time, "cinder cones" build up around pin holes in
the coatings. As the size of pin holes decreases, the
voltage that is applied to the cell to maintain the cur-
rent at the desired level eventually reaches the com-
pliance voltage of the potentiostat/galvanostat (? 100
V, in our case), and spark discharges are observed at
To show how the mass of CaHPO, . 2H,O depos-
ited varies with time at different current densities,
plots of log mass versus log time are provided in Fig-
ure 3. At high and at low current densities it appears
that a limit to the amount of material that can be
deposited is approached. At intermediate current
densities, limits to the amount of material deposited
were not reached, but the deposits we presently pro-
duce become visibly nonuniform when the coverages
exceed approximately 10 mg/cm2. At lower coverages
the coatings are uniform to the unaided eye. Finally,
because conventional micrographs provide limited
morphological information, we made stereoscopic
images, shown in Figure 4, of a coating produced by
passing 20 mA/cm2 for 250 seconds (5 C/cm2). This
same current density ultimately was used to coat the
passed at several different current densities.
Current efficiency as a function of total charge
porous plugs for the animal study to be described
Conversion of brushite coatings to
Since the early 1950s calcium phosphates, particu-
larly hydroxyapatite, have been used extensively for
protein separations. Unfortunately, precipitation of
hydroxyapatite in a single step from separate solu-
tions containing calcium and phosphate generally
produces a finely divided solid that is not suitable for
chromatographic purposes because the flow rates
through it are prohibitively low. In 1956 Tiselius et
a1.” described a procedure in which crystalline
brushite was prepared and then coated to hydroxy-
apatite by boiling for one hour in an aqueous one
molar solution of sodium hydroxide. Greatly im-
proved solvent flow rates were observed through hy-
droxyapatite prepared in this manner because the
macroscopic structure of the hydroxyapatite was sim-
ilar to that of the brushite from which it was derived.
A number of modifications to the basic procedure of
Tiselius et al. have been made with the goal of devis-
ing materials with improved chromatographic prop-
ertie~.”-’~ We have adapted these procedures to con-
vert electrolytically deposited brushite coatings to hy-
droxyapatite. Two extremes in the conditions for
affecting the brushite-to-hydroxyapatite conversions
are described below. In one extreme, brushite-coated
devices were heated at 95-100°C in 0.1M NaOH(aq)
for 18-24 h. These conditions, which are much
harsher than those used by Tiselius et al., were cho-
sen to assure that the desired conversion proceeded
in high yield on the surface of the implants. A typical
X-ray powder diffraction pattern for hydroxyapatite
produced in this manner is shown in Figure 5. Good
Figure 3. Mass accumulation as a function of time at sev-
eral current densities. (a) 50 mA/cm2, (b) 20 mA/cm2, [cl 10
mA/cm2, (d) 5 mA/crni, 1.) 2 mA/cm2,‘ (Q 1 mA/cm2.
brushite coating on uolished 316L stainless steel.
Stereoscopic scanning electron micrographs of
BRUSHITE AND HYDROXYAPATITE COATINGS
Figure 5. XRD pattern for hydroxyapatite prepared by heating brushite at 95100°C in 0.1M NaOH(aq).
agreement is obtained with a reference for hydroxy-
apatite shown in the inset.'* No extraneous peaks are
present nor are any of the expected peaks missing
from the diffraction pattern although it appears that
our sample and the reference sample have somewhat
different preferred crystal orientations. The peaks at
20 = 38.472" and 20 = 44.738" are due to the alumi-
num sample holder. Sodium analyses of hydroxyap-
atite coatings prepared in this manner show that the
product contains less than 3% sodium by weight.
A much milder treatment also proved to be suc-
cessful in converting the brushite coatings to hy-
droxyapatite coatings. Figure 6 shows a powder dif-
fraction pattern for a brushite sample that was con-
verted to hydroxyapatite by being exposed to 0.1M
NaOH(aq) for 72 h at room temperature. Again, good
agreement is obtained with the reference XRD pat-
tern for hydroxyapatite, and analyses by atomic ab-
sorption indicate that the sodium concentration in
these samples is less than 1.5%. Note, however, that
the peaks in Figure 6 are significantly broader than
those observed for samples prepared at 95-100°C
(Fig. 5). As is frequently the case when broad peaks
are observed in X-ray powder patterns, the broad
peaks in this pattern are probably due to very small
crystal domains in the sample being analyzed. Alter-
natively, the broad peaks might be attributed to re-
sidual strain or low crystallinity. Crude kinetic stud-
Figure 6. XRD pattern for hydroxyapatite prepared from brushite at room temperature in 0.1M NaOH(aq).
a charge of 5C/cm2 had passed. Figure 7b shows a
hydroxyapatite coating prepared by first depositing
brushite on polished 316L stainless steel using the
same electrolytic conditions. The brushite coating
was then converted to hydroxyapatite by exposing it
to 0.1M NaOH at 95-100°C for 24 h, as described
above. This procedure for converting brushite to hy-
droxyapatite is benign to the metal surface. Except for
a small amount of remodeling that appears to have
occurred for the hydroxyapatite, the macroscopic
structures observed for the brushite and hydroxyap-
atite samples are quite similar. This is entirely consis-
tent with earlier chromatographic precedents for con-
verting brushite to hydroxyapatite. As is desired in
both cases, the conversions are effected with little
overall change in the dimensions of the crystals.
Preliminary comparison of bony ingrowth for
hydroxyapatite-coated, brushite-coated and
uncoated porous metallic implants
We recently completed a small study, using a rab-
bit model, that examined the propensity of electro-
lytically produced CaHPO, . 2H20 and hydroxyapa-
tite coatings to promote bony ingrowth. Because
of the small size of the study, we chose a single
set of electrochemical conditions to produce
CaHPO, * 2H20 coatings and a single procedure to
convert CaHPO, . 2H20 coatings to hydroxyapatite.
A survey of the literature for plasma-sprayed pros-
theses was used to decide upon a surface coverage for
the electrolytically produced coatings. Based on this
survey, we chose to coat the porous plugs used in
this study at 20 mA/cm2 until 5 C/cm2 of charge had
passed (vide supra). Note the position of these con-
ditions in Figures 1-3. Conversion of the brushite
coatings to hydroxyapatite was accomplished by
heating the implants at 95-100°C for 18-24 h.
Figure 7. Scanning electron micrographs of (a) electrolyt-
ically prepared brushite coating on polished 316L stainless
steel, (b) brushite coating on 316L stainless steel after being
ies of the brushite-to-hydroxyapatite conversion are
consistent with this argument. Because the conver-
sion occurs over several days at room temperature, it
is possible to sample the material periodically to mon-
itor the progress of the reaction. After 6 8  h diffrac-
tion patterns indicate that the brushite is dehydrated
to monetite, CaHPO,. After approximately 24 h the
material is ostensibly amorphous, and no resolvable
peaks are present in the diffraction pattern. It is not
until 48-72 h that the broad peaks seen in Figure 6
begin to appear for hydroxyapatite.
Finally, the influence of the brushite-to-hydroxy-
apatite conversion on the macroscopic structure of
the coatings is shown in Figures 7a and 7b. Figure 7a
shows a brushite coating deposited on polished 316L
stainless steel at a current density of 20 mA/cm2 until
Thirty cylindrical porous-coated titanium plugs
measuring 4 mm in diameter by 9 mm in length were
obtained from Intermedics Orthopaedics (Sioux City,
Iowa). Six were coated with hydroxyapatite and six
with brushite, as described above. The remaining
plugs were kept as uncoated controls.
Twelve 3-4 kg New Zealand white rabbits under-
went bilateral placement of coated and control plugs
into the distal femoral metaphysis. Briefly, the lower
extremities of the anesthetized animals were prepped
and a three centimeter incision made over the knee
joints. The skin was retracted and an incision made
into the lateral retinaculum adjacent to the patella.
BRUSHITE AND HYDROXYAPATITE COATINGS
The patellas were dislocated medially to expose the
intercondylar grooves. A small Steinman pin marked
the approximate location of the axis of the femoral
shafts. A 5/32 inch drill hole was made into the med-
ullary canal. One titanium plug was press fit into
each hole and advanced until the distal end lay just
below the articular surface. The patella was relocated
and the wound repaired in layers.
Six animals received hydroxyapatite-coated plugs
on the left side and control plugs on the contralateral
side. Similarly, six animals received brushite-coated
plugs. All animals were sacrificed on day 42. The
paired femurs were harvested and half immersed in
70% ethanol for histological examination. The re-
maining halves were used for mechanical testing.
The ends of the titanium plugs were exposed in the
femoral samples by carefully cutting proximally and
distally using an Isomet low-speed diamond saw
(Buehler, Lake Bluff, IL). Pushout tests were con-
ducted with an Instron Model 1000 using a crosshead
speed of 10 mm/min. Each specimen was tested to
failure, with the right limb control plug being com-
pared to the left coated plug.
After cutting the plug and attached bone longitu-
dinally through the center of the plug, specimens
were processed undecalcified in methacrylate. Dupli-
cate twenty-five micron sections were prepared by
grinding and polishing each half. A Bioquant image
analysis system was used to measure total linear im-
Results of Preliminary Study in New Zealand
*Data given as a percentage of total area of implant cov-
'Values expressed as amount of force in pounds required
plant surface and percent area that had attached
Table I lists results for bony ingrowth and mechan-
ical pushout of the paired implants. Two rabbits in
the hydroxyapatite and one in the brushite group
were eliminated due to improper placement of the
implants. Evaluation of the percent bony ingrowth
revealed a significant increase in hydroxyapatite-
coated implants versus uncoated ( p  < 0.018) or
brushite-coated ( p  < 0.035). There were too few num-
bers to evaluate the mechanical strength statistically.
While the size of this study was quite small, the re-
sults are encouraging and have led us to begin exam-
ining the performance of electrolytically coated hip
prostheses (Zimmer) in large dogs. The results of this
dog study will be the focus of a future p~blication.~~
